    46	   to `submitted`, presenting its claim token and a result (§2–§4
    47	   below). A submitted task then advances to `completed` or `failed`
    48	   per the rules in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.
    49	5. **Release on reclaim.** If the task store reclaims the task
    50	   before submit (task store policy per
    51	   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5), the worker's
    52	   token is invalidated. A worker that discovers its token has been
    53	   invalidated MUST NOT subsequently attempt to submit against that
    54	   task; it MUST discard any partial result.
    55	
    56	Integrator behavior does **not** follow this worker-role lifecycle.
    57	The integrator is event-driven and is defined separately in §5 below
    58	and in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
    59	
    60	### 1.1 What a worker MAY assume about its inputs
    61	
    62	Before a task is dispatched to a worker-role queue, the orchestrator
    63	MUST ensure the task's payload references (proposal, trial,
    64	parent commits) satisfy the dispatch preconditions listed per role
    65	below. A worker MAY therefore rely on those preconditions and is not
    66	required to re-validate them. A worker that nevertheless discovers a
    67	violation (e.g. a referenced proposal has been retracted) MUST fail
    68	the task with an error status; it MUST NOT repair store state
    69	itself.
    70	
    71	### 1.2 What a worker MUST NOT do
    72	
    73	- A worker MUST NOT write to any protocol-owned object other than
    74	  the task it holds a claim on and the outputs that task's role is
    75	  defined to produce (§2–§4).
    76	- A worker MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*`);
    77	  that is the integrator's exclusive authority
    78	  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9,
    79	  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).
    80	- A worker MUST NOT advance a task's state beyond `submitted`.
    81	  Transitions to `completed` or `failed` are the orchestrator's
    82	  responsibility ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4).

138	### 2.4 Submission
   139	
   140	When its work on a `plan` task is complete, the planner MUST submit
   141	the task (§1 step 4). The submission payload MUST include:
   142	
   143	- `proposal_ids` — the identifiers of every proposal the planner
   144	  created under this task, in the order the planner considers most
   145	  preferred. MAY be empty.
   146	- `status` — one of `"success"` (the planner completed normally,
   147	  regardless of proposal count) or `"error"` (the planner could
   148	  not complete; any partially-written proposals MUST remain in
   149	  `"drafting"` state and MUST NOT be dispatched).
   150	
   151	A planner MUST NOT submit a `plan` task while any of its
   152	referenced proposals is still in `"drafting"` state.
   153	
   154	## 3. Implementer
   155	
   156	The implementer realizes a proposal as a working-tree change on a

171	### 3.2 Outputs
   172	
   173	The implementer produces a **trial** object
   174	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:
   175	
   176	1. Persist a trial with `status == "starting"`,
   177	   `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and
   178	   `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any
   179	   repository write observable to other roles.
   180	2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*`
   181	   ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the
   182	   proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to
   183	   this branch name.
   184	3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the
   185	   tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and
   186	   `description`.
   187	
   188	The implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are
   189	owned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial
   190	MUST NOT be set by the implementer.
   191	
   192	### 3.3 Worker-branch invariants
   193	
   194	- The `work/*` branch MUST be unique to this trial. Two trials
   195	  MUST NOT share a worker branch name.
   196	- Every commit on the worker branch MUST be reachable from the
   197	  declared `parent_commits`. An implementer MUST NOT introduce
   198	  commits whose history does not descend from the proposal's
   199	  declared parents.
   200	- The implementer MAY produce multiple commits on the worker
   201	  branch. The evaluator consumes only the tip (`commit_sha`); the
   202	  integrator's squash rule is defined in
   203	  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
   204	
   205	### 3.4 Submission
   206	
   207	The implementer submits with:
   208	
   209	- `trial_id` — the trial it created.
   210	- `status` — one of:
   211	  - `"success"` — the `work/*` branch tip is `commit_sha` and is
   212	    ready to be evaluated.
   213	  - `"error"` — the implementer could not realize the proposal.
   214	    The trial MUST be persisted with `status == "error"`. No
   215	    evaluate task is dispatched against an errored trial.
   216	- `commit_sha` — required when `status == "success"`; MUST equal
   217	  the tip of the worker branch.
   218	
   219	A resubmission of the same `implement` task MUST be idempotent: a
   220	duplicate submit presenting the same `trial_id` and `commit_sha`
   221	MUST be accepted without side effect. A duplicate submit that
   222	disagrees with the already-recorded result MUST be rejected
   223	([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2).

35	### 1.2 Transitions
    36	
    37	The permitted transitions are exactly:
    38	
    39	```text
    40	          claim                 submit                accept
    41	pending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed
    42	   ▲                 │                       │
    43	   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject
    44	   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed
    45	                                             │
    46	```
    47	
    48	- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim
    49	  token. §3.
    50	- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the
    51	  current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload.
    52	  Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker
    53	  declares success or failure. §4.
    54	- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`.
    55	  Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.
    56	- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated.
    57	  §4.3.
    58	- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated,
    59	  including when the worker's own submission declared failure.
    60	  §4.3.
    61	
    62	Any transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store.
    63	A conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts
    64	as serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the
    65	others MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller
    66	distinguish "invalid" from "raced".
    67

151	## 4. Submit
   152	
   153	### 4.1 Submission operation
   154	
   155	A submit operation requires:
   156	
   157	- The `task_id`.
   158	- The current claim `token`.
   159	- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind`
   160	  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).
   161	
   162	A successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` (or, under the
   163	conditions in §4.3, `claimed → failed`) atomically with persisting
   164	the result payload.
   165	
   166	### 4.2 Idempotency
   167	
   168	A resubmission — a second submit carrying the same token as an
   169	earlier successful submit — MUST be handled as follows:
   170	
   171	- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to
   172	  the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and
   173	  MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. "Content
   174	  equivalence" means the normative fields identified per role
   175	  agree:
   176	  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (as a set, not a sequence)
   177	    and `status`.
   178	  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha`
   179	    (when present).
   180	  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as
   181	    JSON values; key order does not matter).
   182	- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent,
   183	  the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is
   184	  the committed result.
   185	
   186	This rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a
   187	network or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or
   188	corrupting the recorded result.
   189	
   190	### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal
   191	
   192	A submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions
   193	to a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:
   194	
   195	- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's
   196	  `status` is `"success"` and the result satisfies the role's
   197	  success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a
   198	  reachable `commit_sha`).
   199	- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:
   200	  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed
   201	    payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).
   202	  - The worker itself declared failure:
   203	    - `plan` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`.
   204	    - `implement` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`.
   205	    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`
   206	      AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to
   207	      `"error"`.
   208	    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "eval_error"` →
   209	      `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain
   210	      `"starting"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad;
   211	      only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh
   212	      `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the
   213	      protocol does not mandate automatic retry.
   214	
   215	Every terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event
   216	in the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)). The
   217	orchestrator MAY implement the `claimed → submitted → completed`
   218	(or `failed`) sequence as two distinct persisted states or as a
   219	single store transaction whose event stream includes both
   220	transitions, but subscribers MUST observe both events in order.
   221	
   222	### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited
   223	
   224	Once a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its
   225	task fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task
   226	store chooses to expose outside the normative schema
   227	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a
   228	resubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of
   229	content equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal
   230	transition is no longer valid.
   231

244	### 4.2 Outputs
   245	
   246	The evaluator MUST:
   247	
   248	1. Produce a `metrics` object whose keys are a subset of the
   249	   declared `metrics_schema` keys and whose values satisfy the
   250	   per-metric type rules
   251	   ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3, §7.2).
   252	2. Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs,
   253	   diagnostic files) and populate the trial's `artifacts_uri`.
   254	3. Set the trial's `completed_at` timestamp.
   255	
   256	The evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any other
   257	protocol-owned mutable state.
   258	
   259	### 4.3 Non-interference
   260	
   261	The evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip
   262	but MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT
   263	write to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation
   264	(test caches, build outputs) MUST be local to the evaluator's own
   265	workspace.
   266	
   267	### 4.4 Submission
   268	
   269	The evaluator submits with:
   270	
   271	- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.
   272	- `status` — one of:
   273	  - `"success"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.
   274	  - `"error"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons
   275	    attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test
   276	    failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial
   277	    metrics.
   278	  - `"eval_error"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons
   279	    unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault,
   280	    evaluator bug). The trial's final status remains `starting`
   281	    until reconciled; the task protocol treats this as a task
   282	    failure and permits redispatch per
   283	    [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3.
   284	- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent
   285	  when `status == "eval_error"`.
   286	
   287	Resubmission is idempotent under the same rules as §3.4: identical
   288	`trial_id` + `metrics` + `status` MUST be accepted; inconsistent
   289	resubmission MUST be rejected.
   290

308	## 7. Proposal and trial lifecycle interactions
   309	
   310	The task state machine is not the only lifecycle in the system.
   311	Proposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1) and trials
   312	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7) have their own state
   313	fields. The task protocol interacts with these lifecycles at two
   314	points:
   315	
   316	- **Plan submit.** A successful plan-task submission MAY reference
   317	  proposals that have been transitioned to `state == "ready"` by
   318	  the planner ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2). The task
   319	  protocol does not itself transition proposal state; it treats
   320	  the proposal as a value produced by the planner.
   321	- **Implement submit.** A successful implement-task submission
   322	  requires a trial created and advanced by the implementer
   323	  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.2). The orchestrator, on
   324	  accepting the submission, MAY transition the referenced
   325	  proposal's `state` from `dispatched` to `completed`. Whether to
   326	  do so is left to the orchestrator's higher-level logic; the
   327	  task protocol does not mandate it.
   328	- **Evaluate submit.** A successful evaluate-task submission
   329	  populates the trial's `metrics`, `completed_at`, and (per
   330	  worker-declared status) the trial's `status`. The integrator's
   331	  subsequent promotion of the trial into the canonical lineage is
   332	  out of this chapter's scope
   333	  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).

232	## 5. Reclamation
   233	
   234	### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted
   235	
   236	A conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from
   237	`claimed` or `submitted` back to `pending` — in any of the
   238	following cases:
   239	
   240	- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed and the task is not
   241	  in a terminal state.
   242	- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.
   243	- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker
   244	  is unreachable.
   245	
   246	A task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.
   247	
   248	### 5.2 Effect of reclamation
   249	
   250	Reclamation:
   251	
   252	1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations
   253	   presenting it MUST be rejected.
   254	2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.
   255	3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.
   256	4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log
   257	   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically
   258	   with the state change.
   259	
   260	Any partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is
   261	no longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker
   262	created store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting
   263	trials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are
   264	subject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not
   265	delete them.
   266	
   267	### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation
   268	
   269	A worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its
   270	task's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its
   271	token has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task
   272	and MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).
   273	
   274	### 5.4 No implicit reclamation from `submitted`
   275	
   276	A task that has reached `submitted` MUST be processed by the
   277	orchestrator (transition to `completed` or `failed`) rather than
   278	silently reclaimed. Reclamation from `submitted` is permitted only
   279	under the explicit conditions in §5.1; in particular, automatic
   280	`expires_at`-based reclamation SHOULD NOT apply to `submitted`
   281	tasks, since the worker has already discharged its obligation.
   282

159	### 3.2 State values
   160	
   161	The `state` field MUST be one of `"pending"`, `"claimed"`,
   162	`"submitted"`, `"completed"`, `"failed"`. The legal transitions
   163	between these states are defined in
   164	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The data model
   165	constrains only that the value be one of the listed strings.
   166	
   167	### 3.3 Payload shape
   168	
   169	The `payload` object's shape depends on `kind`.
   170	
   171	- `"plan"` — MUST contain `experiment_id` (string). MAY contain
   172	  planner-specific hints.
   173	- `"implement"` — MUST contain `proposal_id` (string) referring to
   174	  a proposal with `state == "ready"` at the time of dispatch.
   175	- `"evaluate"` — MUST contain `trial_id` (string) referring to a
   176	  trial with `status == "starting"` and a `commit_sha` set.
   177	
   178	### 3.4 Claim object
   179	
   180	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   181	|---|---|---|---|
   182	| `token` | yes | string | Opaque unforgeable claim token. |
   183	| `worker_id` | yes | string | Opaque identifier for the claiming worker. |
   184	| `claimed_at` | yes | timestamp | When the claim was issued. |
   185	| `expires_at` | no | timestamp | When the task store MAY reclaim. |
   186	
   187	The `claim` field MUST be present when `state == "claimed"`, and MUST
   188	NOT be present for any other state. The schema in
   189	[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json) enforces this
   190	mutual exclusion.

281	### 7.1 Fields
   282	
   283	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   284	|---|---|---|---|
   285	| `trial_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier. |
   286	| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this trial belongs to. |
   287	| `proposal_id` | yes | string | The proposal that produced this trial. |
   288	| `status` | yes | string | One of `"starting"`, `"success"`, `"error"`, `"eval_error"`. |
   289	| `parent_commits` | yes | array of SHA | Inherited from the proposal. |
   290	| `branch` | no | string | Worker branch under `work/*`. Present once the implementer starts. |
   291	| `commit_sha` | no | string | Worker-branch tip SHA. Present once the implementer completes. |
   292	| `trial_commit_sha` | no | string | Canonical-lineage SHA under `trial/*`. Present once the integrator has promoted the trial. |
   293	| `artifacts_uri` | no | string (URI) | Where the trial's artifacts live. |
   294	| `description` | no | string | Human-readable summary. |
   295	| `metrics` | no | object | Metrics payload; shape dictated by the experiment's metrics schema. |
   296	| `started_at` | yes | timestamp | When the implementer began. |
   297	| `completed_at` | no | timestamp | When the evaluator submitted. |
   298	
   299	### 7.2 Metrics payload
   300	
   301	If present, `metrics` MUST be an object whose keys are a subset of
   302	the declared metrics-schema keys and whose values match the declared
   303	types (§1.3) or are `null`. Because the metrics-schema is
   304	per-experiment, [`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json)
   305	cannot express this constraint generically and leaves `metrics` as
   306	an open object; the per-metric type check is a runtime responsibility
   307	of the orchestrator. A conforming orchestrator MUST reject a metrics
   308	payload that violates the experiment's metrics-schema, and MUST NOT
   309	record the trial as `"success"` in that case.
   310	
   311	### 7.3 Status transitions
   312	
   313	Trial status transitions are behavioral and are defined alongside
   314	the task and event protocols in
   315	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The data model
   316	constrains only that the status be one of the listed strings.

11	An **experiment** is a single directed-code-evolution run. It has:
    12	
    13	- A starting commit on a designated branch of a git repository.
    14	- An **objective** — a scalar function of a trial's measured metrics,
    15	  to be either maximized or minimized.
    16	- A declared **metrics schema** — the names and types of the metrics
    17	  evaluators will report.
    18	- Role configurations — how to run the planner, implementer, and
    19	  evaluator for this experiment.
    20	- Termination conditions (trial-count, wall-clock, convergence
    68	A **proposal** is the planner's output: a human-readable description
    69	of a change plus the metadata required to dispatch work against it.
    70	Schema: [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json).
    71	Proposals have a lifecycle (drafting → ready → dispatched →
    72	completed) that is defined in
    73	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter treats a
    74	proposal as a value object; the task protocol defines how its
    75	lifecycle advances.
    77	## 4. Trial
    78	
    79	A **trial** is a single attempt to improve the objective. A trial
    80	references:
    81	
    82	- The proposal that was implemented.
    83	- The resulting commit(s) on the worker branch and the squashed
    84	  commit on the canonical branch.
    85	- The metrics reported by the evaluator.
    86	- A status (starting, success, error, eval_error).
    87	
    88	Trials are the unit of progress: a terminated experiment is
    89	summarized by the sequence of its completed trials and their scores
    90	under the objective. Schema:
    91	[`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json).

82	An experiment config is the declarative input to an experiment. It
    83	is authored by a user (or generated by tooling) and persisted
    84	alongside the experiment.
    85	
    86	The protocol defines only the **host-mechanism-independent core** of
    87	the config — fields that every conforming implementation needs to
    88	observe the same semantics. How roles are bound (local subprocess,
    89	HTTP service, in-process adapter, …) is outside this chapter's
    90	scope; role binding is specified alongside the role contracts in
    91	[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) (Phase 2). Until then, implementations
    92	MAY carry role-binding information as additional top-level fields
    93	without violating this chapter.

**Findings**
- Bug — [04-task-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md:162), §4.1 says: “A successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` (or, under the conditions in §4.3, `claimed → failed`)”. But [04-task-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md:37), §1.2 says the permitted transitions are exactly `claimed → submitted` and then `submitted → failed`. That is a direct contradiction in the state machine.

- Bug — [03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:41), §1 step 3 says: “The worker MAY report progress; it MUST NOT mutate any protocol-owned object other than the task it holds a claim on.” That conflicts with required role outputs in [03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:173), §3.2 (“Persist a trial...”) and [03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:246), §4.2 (“Set the trial’s `completed_at` timestamp”). The evaluator section then repeats the problem in [03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:256): “The evaluator MUST NOT modify... any other protocol-owned mutable state.”

- Bug — [02-data-model.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/02-data-model.md:187), §3.4 says: “The `claim` field MUST be present when `state == "claimed"`, and MUST NOT be present for any other state.” The schema enforces that in [task.schema.json](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/schemas/task.schema.json:102). But [04-task-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md:168), §4.2 defines idempotent resubmit as “a second submit carrying the same token as an earlier successful submit”, and [04-task-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md:240), §5.1 allows `submitted` reclamation based on “`claim.expires_at`”. Once the task is `submitted`, the normative object no longer carries the claim or its expiry, so the spec does not define what remains authoritative.
